denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request your provider submitted on May 18, 2026 for a surveillance MRI of the pituitary (sella) with and without contrast to monitor your pituitary microadenoma (prolactinoma) (D35.2). After review, this request is not approved at this time.

Our records show that the prior authorization previously issued for your surveillance imaging has lapsed. Continuation of this surveillance imaging requires an active prior authorization, and a new authorization request with current clinical information must be submitted before the study can be approved. Because an active authorization is not on file, the requested study is not authorized.

In reviewing the request, we applied the pediatric neuroimaging appropriateness criteria referenced in the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB), under which routine surveillance at the requested interval is not supported. On that basis, and in the absence of an active authorization, the request does not meet our criteria for approval.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the clinical criteria used in this review. If you disagree with this decision, you may submit a written appeal within 180 days of the date of this notice and include updated clinical documentation. Please refer to your plan documents for instructions on how to submit your appeal.

Sincerely,
Aetna Prior Authorization Review
clinical_context: The member is a 21-year-old woman with a known pituitary microadenoma (a prolactinoma) measuring approximately 6 mm, diagnosed in 2024 after she presented with galactorrhea and a serum prolactin of 92 ng/mL. She is maintained on cabergoline 0.5 mg twice weekly and enrolled in a standard surveillance program with periodic MRI of the sella to monitor adenoma size and treatment response, as recommended in adult Endocrine Society guidance. Her prior surveillance MRI was authorized and completed; the current request is the scheduled follow-up study. The prior authorization for ongoing surveillance lapsed administratively, but her provider submitted current prolactin levels and a statement of continued medical need with this request, so the lapse is clerical rather than clinical. The pediatric neuroimaging appropriateness criteria the insurer applied do not apply to her, because she is a 21-year-old adult and adult pituitary surveillance guidance supports continued interval imaging. The denial notice also omits the ERISA disclosures of her appeal and civil-action rights that apply to her employer-sponsored plan.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Aetna, Prior Authorization, imaging, 18-25, F, continuation_of_care_lapsed); dx/tx are credible imaging fits; denial_rationale_seed describes continuation_of_care_lapsed (lapsed PA, new auth required); age 21 in band; not 71+ so null employer_archetype fine.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Surveillance MRI for a treated prolactinoma is standard adult endocrine practice; Aetna requires active PA for surveillance imaging; the lapsed-PA continuation friction is real; the pediatric-criteria misapplication to a 21-year-old and the omitted ERISA disclosure on a self-funded plan are authentic exploitable gaps; drug/dx/age epidemiologically apt.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Correctly invokes the Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) tell with cool procedural cadence and no wrong-insurer fingerprints, but the cited 'pediatric neuroimaging appropriateness criteria' applied to an adult is an off-key reference that slightly cracks the voice illusion. (This mismatch is the intended incorrect_demographic_guideline flaw.)
insurer_voice.improvement: Off-key pediatric reference is the deliberate demographic-mismatch flaw, not a voice defect to remove.

denial_logic.reasoning: The continuation-of-care logic is authentically shoddy and on-tactic (lapsed auth, circular 'not on file'), but the second paragraph cites pediatric neuroimaging criteria for an adult pituitary case, an ill-fitting guideline that the critic penalizes. The mismatch is the intended incorrect_demographic_guideline flaw, deliberately discoverable.
denial_logic.improvement: Pediatric-criteria mismatch is the intended flaw; retained for discoverability.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Galactorrhea + prolactin 92 ng/mL supports a microadenoma; 6 mm correctly qualifies as a microadenoma; cabergoline 0.5 mg twice weekly is a real first-line regimen; periodic sella MRI with and without contrast is correct adult surveillance protocol; age 21 premenopausal woman fits prolactinoma epidemiology; no invented drugs or impossible courses.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Pituitary-protocol MRI with and without contrast is the established modality for monitoring pituitary adenomas including prolactinomas; for a microadenoma under surveillance, periodic interval MRI is precisely indicated; exact category match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: No neighbour involves a pituitary/sella indication or prolactinoma surveillance; closest by modality are brain/orbit MRIs (different anatomy, dx, and one-time trajectory); continuation_of_care_lapsed flaw axis is unique among neighbours. Not a near-duplicate.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.071 (nearest=case_185_aetna_mednec), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: Routine endocrine surveillance imaging for a benign adenoma; no SI/self-harm/child abuse/IPV/EOL; no gender-affirming care or dysphoria framing (under-26 trigger does not apply); employer-sponsored commercial Aetna plan, no Medicare/Medicaid. No banned topics.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Age (21F), dx (prolactinoma D35.2), and requested surveillance sella MRI agree across letter and context; denial grounds (lapsed PA, pediatric criteria) are consistent within the letter; the appealable falsehoods (pediatric criteria on an adult, clerical lapse treated as clinical) are allowed denial-vs-fact tension; timeline (2024 dx, prior MRI, lapse, current follow-up) coherent; no facts unstable.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Formal bureaucratic insurer boilerplate and dense clinical recitation; no 'As an AI' tics, GPT vocabulary, listicles, or marketing filler; nothing at the >=0.7 conspicuous-tell bar.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural Aetna register with circular justification and vague CPB appeal, impersonal sign-off, no apology/marketing; clinical context neutral plain prose stating even the lapse clinically ('clerical rather than clinical') without emotional pleading.

financial_auditor.reasoning: No member out-of-pocket figure or dollar liability; only 'at no charge' for criteria access; silence on cost liability is standard practice; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation.

legal_auditor.reasoning: Cites only the applicable CPB without inventing statutes; states a concrete 180-day written-appeal right but omits ERISA civil-action/§502(a) and external-review disclosures (the intended realistic procedural-omission flaw, an attack vector not a defect); no Medicare framing on this commercial self-funded plan.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 21F with a prolactinoma (most common in women of reproductive age, classic galactorrhea + elevated prolactin) is epidemiologically apt; consistent female pronouns; no stereotyping. The pediatric-criteria misapplication is a policy flaw, not a demographic-validity defect.

scope_guard.reasoning: Aetna in-scope, Prior Authorization, self_funded employer-sponsored commercial (no Medicare/Medicaid/TRICARE/VA/marketplace), adult 21, member-facing written appeal only, no live-filing/attorney/court language.

date_sanity.reasoning: Request May 18, 2026; 180-day appeal window sensible; diagnosis pinned to 2024 with a prior authorized/completed MRI preceding the current follow-up; coherent 2024-2026 stack; denial follows the request; no impossible durations or vague hedges.

citation_traceability.reasoning: References 'the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB)' generically with no bulletin number/edition/page and no fabricated alphanumeric; 'pediatric neuroimaging appropriateness criteria' is a descriptive category, not an over-specified citation; offers criteria on request. Realistic umbrella vagueness.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Insurer applied pediatric neuroimaging criteria to a 21-year-old adult (wrong-population guideline); omitted required ERISA appeal/civil-action disclosures on an employer-sponsored plan; the lapse is clerical and the provider already submitted current prolactin levels and a statement of need; patient meets adult standard of care. Only the lapsed-auth technicality defends, undercut by the contemporaneous submission. Easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Insurer applied pediatric neuroimaging appropriateness criteria to a 21-year-old adult, where adult pituitary surveillance guidance supports continued imaging
Denial notice omits required ERISA appeal and civil-action rights disclosures for the employer-sponsored plan
The authorization lapse is purely clerical and the provider already submitted current prolactin levels and a statement of continued medical need
strong_defenses: An active prior authorization had genuinely lapsed, giving a facially valid administrative ground to require resubmission
